item.title: In praise of tech troublemakers
item.description: As CEOs refuse constraints, workers feel a responsibility to prevent AI’s most dangerous uses

item.title: How the Trump-Xi threats of trade war softened into a quieter rivalry
item.description: The forthcoming US-China leaders’ summit will be much less fraught than the last one

item.title: The problematic inevitability of Andy Burnham
item.description: Labour’s assumptions about its leader-in-waiting are logical but no guarantee of success

item.title: Welcome our new robotaxi overlords
item.description: The arrival of driverless vehicles is a second chance for cities

item.title: How to help left-behind places
item.description: Better data, industrial policy and education could all ameliorate inequalities

item.title: Can’t cook, won’t cook? Start by feeding yourself
item.description: A cure for kitchen nerves, plus a recipe for anchovy pasta

item.title: The energy crisis is no excuse for bad subsidies
item.description: We know what worked and what failed the last time around

item.title: The Fed’s balancing act
item.description: Plus tech earnings

item.title: UAExit adds momentum to the Opec doom loop
item.description: The Emirates’ departure reflects the cartel’s declining market share and the ongoing energy transition

item.title: Does trade cause peace? Ask an economist
item.description: Researchers have been trawling history to measure the pacifying effects of moving goods around

item.title: AI companies are just companies
item.description: As we leap into a new technological age, the old rules of capitalism still apply

item.title: Big Tech’s earnings get ever bigger, and ever less useful
item.description: Meta, Alphabet and peers are growing smartly, but their value hinges on hard-to-answer questions about AI supremacy

item.title: The twilight of Opec
item.description: UAE’s departure leaves Saudi Arabia trying to hold together a fractious cartel

item.title: GSK’s game of catch-up might just work
item.description: Drugmaker is looking to see which of its pipeline of drugs can be pushed forward while also trying to buy time

item.title: Lessons for countries on the edge
item.description: Africa’s demographics are favourable, but leaders must prepare for future shocks

item.title: Warsh can bring a much-needed trade-off on rates to the Fed
item.description: The designate chair has signalled a willingness to take the central bank’s balance sheet into account when setting monetary policy

item.title: UBS’s bumper profit both helps and hinders its case on capital rules
item.description: Long fight over just how well fortified its balance sheet needs to be is inching in Swiss bank’s direction

item.title: The problem with falling house prices
item.description: Homes are too expensive in the UK, but a large correction would be bad for everyone — including first-time buyers

item.title: Can US trading partners still rely on the rule of law?
item.description: When it comes to settling trade disputes, the Trump administration wants to play judge, jury and executioner

item.title: Australia’s drive to get more blood flowing
item.description: The country has more recreational beekeepers than regular donors and is forced to rely on imports

item.title: King Charles, America and the futility of growth
item.description: The UK’s stagnant economy and the US’s dynamic one are both politically dysfunctional

item.title: Opec without the UAE
item.description: Plus, gilts hit rough water

item.title: UAE opens up an Opec fissure
item.description: Exit represents a rejection of the production-control system dominated in recent years by Saudi Arabia

item.title: Iran war oil price windfall gives Shell a chance to edge ahead of BP
item.description: Extra funds mean majors can now in theory increase exploration without cutting shareholder returns

item.title: It’s never a good idea to sack the entire National Science Board
item.description: Donald Trump is spurning the research that makes progress possible
